coredata.dc:title: A novel CDs coated polyoxometalate/metal-organic framework composite for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Hao
Chen, Yuan
Wu, Ning
Peng, Liping
Yang, Fan
Pan, Zhilan
Liu, Bingqing
Zhang, Hao
Li, Cuiliu
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Considering the high electrical conductivity of carbon dots (CDs) and good oxidation-reduction property of polyoxometalates (POMs), a novel CDs coated POMs /metal organic frameworks (POMOFs) composition structure, CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, was prepared by hydrothermal reaction in this work. PMo12 was encapsulated in the Ni-MOF framework to form the POMOF composite material, which realized the fixation of water-soluble [PMo12O40]3- (PMo12). A comparison study on the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) of the resulting CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF with different compositions revealed that 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF was better formulation in terms of specific capacity (748.8 C·g−1 at 1 A·g−1) and rate performance (75.93 % at 10 A·g−1). Furthermore, a hybrid supercapacitor device was assembled using 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF as positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as negative electrode, which showed an high energy density of 39.22 Wh·kg−1 under 749.9 W·kg−1 and excellent cyclic stability (98.4 % for 20000 cycles at 5 A·g−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF
Polyoxometalates
Metal-organic frameworks
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, CDs, and 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, PMo12, PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF.
XPS survey spectra of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and (b-d) high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p, Mo 3d, C 1 s, O 1 s and P 2p respectively.
SEM images of Ni-MOF (a), PMo12/Ni-MOF (b), 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF (c) and CDs (d), and EDS elemental mapping of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF(e).
(a) TEM images of the 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, (b) HRTEM images of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, (c) SAED pattern of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF.

Electrochemical performances of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrodes measured in 6.0 M KOH solution. (a) CV curves of five materials at 30 mV·s−1. (b) GCD curves of the five materials at 1 A·g−1. (c) Specific capacitance as a function of current density for five electrode materials. (d) Nyquist plots of the five materials (the insets show the high frequency region curve and the fitted equivalent circuit diagram of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF).

(a) CV curve of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode material at different scan rates. (b) GCD plots of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF at various current densities. (c) Cycling performance of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF (The insets show the GCD profiles of the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles). (d) The relationship between the peak current (i p) and the power of one-half of the scan rate (v 1/2). (e) The capacitive contributions at a scan rate of 5 mV·s−1, (f) the relationship between capacitive and diffusion contributions.

Electrochemical performances of the hybrid supercapacitor 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF//AC 6.0 M KOH. (a) Assembly diagram. (b) CV curve of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and AC electrode materials at 10 mV·s−1 in three-electrode mode. (c) CV curves under different potential windows at a scan rate of 20 mV·s−1. (d) CV curve from 10 mV·s−1 to 200 mV·s−1. (e) GCD curve under different current densities. (f) Energy density and power density. (g) Cycle performance at 10 A·g−1 (The insets show GCD profiles of the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles).
Schematic diagram of the formation of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF.
Performance comparison of the prepared electrode material with reported results.

head.ce:title: A novel CDs coated polyoxometalate/metal-organic framework composite for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE; Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province; College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, P R China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the high electrical conductivity of carbon dots (CDs) and good oxidation-reduction property of polyoxometalates (POMs), a novel CDs coated POMs /metal organic frameworks (POMOFs) composition structure, CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, was prepared by hydrothermal reaction in this work. PMo12 was encapsulated in the Ni-MOF framework to form the POMOF composite material, which realized the fixation of water-soluble [PMo12O40]3- (PMo12). A comparison study on the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) of the resulting CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF with different compositions revealed that 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF was better formulation in terms of specific capacity (748.8 C·g−1 at 1 A·g−1) and rate performance (75.93 % at 10 A·g−1). Furthermore, a hybrid supercapacitor device was assembled using 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF as positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as negative electrode, which showed an high energy density of 39.22 Wh·kg−1 under 749.9 W·kg−1 and excellent cyclic stability (98.4 % for 20000 cycles at 5 A·g−1).

ce:section.ce:para: Exploring high-performance and environment-friendly energy storage devices to solve the energy and environmental hazard has been a worldwide research object [1–3]. Supercapacitors have been widely researched as electrical energy storage devices because of their high power density, excellent cycling life and fast charge-discharge rate [4]. However, due to the low capacity of electrode materials, the energy density is far from meeting the application requirements of large-scale energy storage systems, so it is urgent to find electrode materials with superior performance.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have a high specific surface area, ordered pore structure, functional design and adjustable structure, which make the MOFs become an efficient electrode material of the supercapacitor [5]. Recently, MOFs have served as both self-sacrificial templates and metal precursors. They are widely used in the preparation of transition metal sulfides, carbon-based nanomaterials (nitrides, phosphates and oxides), or their composites for supercapacitor electrode materials. Anjali et al. have reported the electrode materials by MOF-derived hollow cage and graphene [6]. Frank et al. converted the Co-MOFs into a porous Co(OH)2 structure [7]. Yamauchi et al. obtained various nanoporous carbons by the carbonization of ZIF-8 [8]. However, the lower conductivity of MOFs-based composite materials limited their wide application in capacitors. Simultaneously, polyoxometalates (POMs), as polyanionic clusters with excellent redox properties, diversity and stability, can serve as anionic templates or structure-directing agents [9]. However, the poor film-forming property and high solubility of POMs in water hinder their use as electrode materials for supercapacitors [10]. Thus, Combining POMs with other insoluble matter is the most valid way to solve these questions. Naturally, the advantages of MOFs and POMs are mingled together. POMs may change the balance between organic ligands and metal cations to influence the reactions, and further control the nucleation and generative process of MOFs and the resulted shape and structure [9,11–13]. Therefore, the controlled synthesis of MOFs nanocrystals can be achieved by using POMs materials to regulate the nucleation and growth process [14]. Recently, polyoxometalate-based MOFs (POMOFs), NENU-n (n = 1–5, POM = [H2SiW12O40]2- (1); [H2GeW12O40]2- (2); [HPW12O40]2- (3); [H2SiMo12O40]2- (4); [HPMo12O40]2- (5); [HAsMo12O40]2- (6)) have been reported [15]. [PMo12O40]3- (PMo12) can accept a large number of electrons just like an “electron sponge”. In order to further improve the conductivity of POM and MOF-based electrode materials, various carbon materials are usually introduced. For example, Lin et al. immobilized polyaniline/FeMo6 nanoparticles on graphene for the pseudocapacitance [16]. Lan prepared a polyoxometalate-based metal-organic framework (POMOFs)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanocomposite for supercapacitor [17].

Carbon dots (CDs) often come from biomasses. It displays versatile advantages, such as high conductivity, high chemical stability, low cost and ultrafine size [18]. So CDs have showed various promising applications, especially in electrochemical energy storage, sensor, solar cell, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis [19]. What’s more, the functional groups on the surface of CDs can be modified with other polymers, organic and inorganic materials. This modification further improves the physical and chemical properties of CDs materials [20]. Interestingly, CDs in nanocomposites enhanced electron transfer and ion transport in the charging and discharging process of the supercapacitor, improved redox reaction, and led to high specific capacitance [21]. Recently related investigations, such as CDs stabilized copper sulfide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide hydrogel [22], polyaniline-carrot derived carbon dots/polypyrrole-graphene nanocomposite [19] and nitrogen-doped carbon dots anchored NiO/Co3O4 ultrathin nanosheets, have been reported in succession [23].

Herein, we used multi-electron PMo12, CDs with high conductivity and MOFs with large specific surface area to build the composites with high conductivity and hierarchical pores. The typical preparation route was illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, the Ni-MOF coated POM composite (POMOF) was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and then the POMOF was modified by CDs of different quality. Importantly, the prepared composites have the following characteristics: (1) PMo12 was packaged in the Ni-MOF framework to avoid dissolution in the electrolyte and improve its electrochemical stability. (2) CDs of a certain quality enhanced electronic conductivity, improved mass transfer, and provided more electrochemical active sites.

The crystalline properties of as-synthesized Ni-MOF, CDs, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF have been identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Fig. S1a showed that the XRD diffraction peaks of the experimentally synthesized Ni-MOF were basically consistent with those of the simulated Ni-MOF. Fig. 1a exhibited that 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF had characteristic peaks of CDs and PMo12/Ni-MOF at the same time, indicating that CDs has been successfully loaded on PMo12/Ni-MOF with a high purity. In addition, (100) diffraction peak of Ni-MOF at 10.62° shifted to 9.80° when PMo12 was introduced, meant that interlayer spacing increased from 8.32 Å to 9.02 Å. It was one of direct evidence that PMo12 was introduced interlayers of the Ni-MOF. In Fig. S1b, with the increase of CDs loading capacity, the peak strength of CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF gradually increased and crystallinity increased. A small part of the peak position variation may be due to the solvent effect [24]. The FT-IR spectra of CDs, PMo12, PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF were shown in Fig. 1b. CDs showed strong absorption bands at 3429, 1629, and 1047 cm−1 attributed to hydroxyl O-H, carboxyl CO and carboxyl C-O-C stretching vibrations respectively. These results demonstrated that CDs had a large content of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. PMo12 showed two strong characteristic absorption bands respectively at 965.7 cm−1 assigned to molybdate ion vibration and 1064 cm−1 attributed to P-O stretching vibration. In the FT-IR spectra of PMo12/Ni-MOF samples, CO stretching vibration band of Ni-MOF at 1638 cm−1 appeared, implying the formation of the complex of Ni with BTC [25], besides characteristic bands of POMs. The observed bands of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF were in agreement with those of PMo12/Ni-MOF and CDs, suggesting successful composition. The N2-adsorption isotherms of Ni-MOF and PMo12/Ni-MOF were displayed in Fig. S2. The BET specific surface area and average pore size of Ni-MOF and PMo12/Ni-MOF were 122 m2·g−1 (5.88 nm) and 112 m2·g−1 (2.838 nm), respectively. The decrease of the pore size and surface area revealed PMo12 particles partially entered mesopores of Ni-MOF.

The surface composition and valence states of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF and 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF were measured using the XPS technique in Fig. 2 and Fig. S3. From Fig. S3b it could be seen that the Ni 2p orbital was electrically disturbed by the addition of PMo12 to the Ni-MOF framework. A positive shift of the Ni 2p3/2 from 855.90 eV (Ni-MOF) to 857.30 eV (PMo12/Ni-MOF) suggested that the Ni node in Ni-MOF bound to the moiety of PMo12, which could be attribute to defect sites in the synthesized MOFs. Fig. 2a showed the XPS full spectrum of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, which proved the existence of Ni, Mo, C, O, and P elements. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the binding energies at 875.25 and 857.65 eV were ascribed to the spin-orbital peaks of Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, while two peaks at 882.25 and 862.50 eV belonged to satellites. Moreover, the binding energy splitting (17.6) was a typical feature of Ni2+ [26], which provided the under-potential active sites for outstanding electrochemical performances [27]. A pair of peaks appeared at 232.67 eV and 235.07 eV in Mo 3d core-level spectrum (Fig. 2c), which could be attributed to Mo6+ 3d5/2 and Mo6+ 3d3/2. It was clear that Mo (VI) was derived from molybdate ions. In Fig. 2d, the C 1 s spectra had three peaks related to C-C/CC (284.89 eV), C-O (286.12 eV), and CO (288.90 eV). However, the corresponding peaks of PMo12/Ni-MOF (Fig. S3d) shifted to lower binding energies, confirming the effective composition of CDs. Furthermore, the CDs were mostly found on the surface of composite materials, implying that a large number of CDs were wrapped on the PMo12/Ni-MOF surface. This structure aided ion diffusion and charge transfer rates and prevented material dissolution and agglomeration, which was beneficial in long-term cycling.

The morphologies of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, CDs were observed by SEM and TEM. The pure Ni-MOF presented a smooth polyhedron ( Fig. 3a). The composite PMo12/Ni-MOF presented a looser polyhedron structure, as shown in Fig. 3b, and a large number of nanoparticles were distributed on surface of the polyhedron, which were conducive to ion diffusion. Encapsulation of PMo12 into MOF framework can effectively avoid its dissolution in electrolytes and improve its electrochemical stability [27]. Fig. 3c showed that in 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF CDs (Fig. 3d) were coated on surface of PMo12/Ni-MOF and formed covered morphology. Introduction of CDs made the surface of the composite material rougher, but didn’t destroy the overall morphology of MOF. In Fig. S4a and b showed morphologies of 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, respectively and it could be seen that too high content of CDs has destroyed primitive morphology of the Ni-MOF. In addition, Ni, Mo, C, P, and O elements were observed to be evenly distributed on the structure in the EDS image as shown in Fig. 3e. However, darker Mo mapping image implied that PMo12 may be covered inside the Ni-MOF. ICP-OES tests showed that the exact ratio of Ni and Mo was 2.21:1 (Table S1).

TEM image of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF ( Fig. 4a) exhibited that the CDs were loaded on the surface of PMo12/Ni-MOF and the PMo12 was perfectly fixed in the Ni-MOF frame. Unbelievably, PMo12/Ni-MOF well retained the covered structure, which was consistent with the SEM results. Furthermore, the HRTEM image as shown in Fig. 4b displayed obvious lattice fringes, in which the 0.853 nm lattice spacing could be ascribed to the (101) interplane spacing of PMo12. The lattice fringe of CDs was mainly distributed at the edge of composite material, indicating that CDs were mainly distributed on the surface of composite material. Some neatly arranged diffraction spots appeared in the selected regional electron diffraction (SAED) image (Fig. 4 c), indicating that the composite had a good crystal structure.

The prepared materials were used as the electrode materials of the supercapacitors, and a series of electrochemical measurements were carried out in 6.0 M KOH. Fig. 5a exhibited a typical cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve comparison of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrodes at 30 mV s−1. All the electrode materials showed obvious redox peaks, reflecting Faradaic behavior. It was observed that the 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode demonstrated the biggest integral area compared to other electrodes, indicating that the manufactured CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode had the best electrochemical performance when the amount of CDs was 6 mg. The GCD curves of the Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode under 1 A·g−1 were shown in Fig. 5b. GCD curves of these five materials presented a house-like shape, which proved the existence of the Faradaic process. The 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode had the longest discharge time and the largest specific capacity values among the five electrodes. According to Equation (S1), the specific capacities of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF were 81.42, 252.3 C·g−1, 343.5, 748.8 and 329.2, respectively. Obviously, the co-existence of CDs, PMo12, and Ni-MOF could improve electrochemical performance. The highest specific capacity of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF may be attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, CDs provided a good interface for the interaction between electrolytes and electrode, promoting electronic transfer, capacity and rate capabilities [28]. Secondly, PMo12 was a proton-rich compound whose reversible multi-electron redox activity contributed to the enhancement of material specific capacity and voltage window. Thirdly, POM anions acted as templates to prevent the self-penetration of polymers during the self-assembly process of nickel ions and organic ligands, so that POM ions existed in the pores of Ni-MOF as charge compensation anions, which further improved the stability of materials [29]. In addition, proper composition and morphology produced an obvious synergistic effect [30]. Unfortunately, when CDs was 8 mg, the integral area of CV curve and specific capacity decreased significantly, indicating that too high CDs amount was not conducive to ion transfer. Fig. 5 c showed the curve of capacity as a function of current density from 1 to 10 A·g−1. When the current density reached 10 A·g−1, the rate performance of Ni-MOF, PMo12/Ni-MOF, 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF were 31.91 %, 59.10 %, 62.42 %, 75.93 % and 74.85 % respectively. Clearly, the specific capacity of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF was higher than those of the other materials at each current density. Furthermore, it was seen that addition of CDs with good conductivity can improve the rate capacity of the material. Fig. 5d showed the Nyquist plots of the synthesized materials, which could be employed to evaluate charge transfer resistance and ion diffusion resistance of the electrode materials. Nyquist plot of each material exhibited a semicircle in the high-frequency range and a straight line in the low-frequency range. The inset showed the enlarged Nyquist plots in the high-frequency region and corresponding equivalent circuits. In the high-frequency region, the intercept of the curve in the x-axis represents the equivalent series resistance (Rs) including the intrinsic resistance of the electrode and electrolyte, the contact resistance at the interfaces of the electrode while the semicircle diameter represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct) [31]. It was obvious that 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF had the smallest equivalent series resistance (Rs) value (0.6398 Ω), which was much lower than those of Ni-MOF (1.6490 Ω) and PMo12/Ni-MOF (1.9989 Ω), which could be ascribed to the fast electron transport channel in 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF. Additionally, the Rct value of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF is 0.7081 Ω, which is less than those of 4CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF (13.19 Ω), 8CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF (15.13 Ω), PMo12/Ni-MOF (12.59 Ω) and Ni-MOF (60.17 Ω). It indicated that 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF not only provided the largest active surface area but also had a strong synergistic effect among CDs, PMo12, and Ni-MOF, which was responsible for outstanding electrochemical performance. Fig. S5 revealed a comparison of the experimental result with the fitting result through the equivalent circuit. The experimental data were basically consistent with the theoretical values, and the Chi-square value of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF was 1.11 × 10−3. In addition, by comparison ( Table 1), it was found that the electrochemical performance of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode material was superior to those of most materials recently reported.

In order to further explore the electrochemical performance of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF, Fig. 6a showed the CV curves at different scan rates from 10 to 100 mV·s−1. The shape of the curve did not change significantly and all showed obvious redox peak with increasing scan rate, which proved the stability and reversibility of the electrode material [40]. As depicted in Fig. 6b, GCD curves of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode at various current densities were all nonlinear and highly symmetric, which were consistent with the CV results. The specific capacity was 748.8, 695.4, 631, 590.7, 568.6 C·g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 8, 10 A·g−1, respectively. Even when the current density was as high as 20 A·g−1, a high specific capacity of 512.4 C·g−1 can still be achieved. Fig. 6c presented its long-term cycling performance under a current density of 10 A·g−1. The capacity retention could reach 90 % after 20000 cycles and the charge-discharge curves of the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles maintained similar shapes (Insets), indicating that the 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF possessed a high structural stability and reversibility. Furthermore, XRD (Fig. S6), XPS (Fig. S7) and SEM (Fig. S8) tests proved that there was not obvious degeneration in the crystal structure and morphology of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF after 20000 cycles. By fitting the linear relationships of the peak currents of cathode and anode versus ν1/2, it was found that the reaction of the 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode material was a diffusion-controlled quasireversible process (Fig. 6d). The relationship between peak current (ip) and scan rate (v) could be calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
i
p
= a
v
b

The values of b for different potentials can be determined from the slopes of the straight lines of logi vs logv, and b ≈ 0.5 indicates a diffusion controlled process corresponding to a battery behavior, while b ≈ 1 refers to a capacitance controlled process with pseudo-capacitance characteristics [41]. Fig. S9 showed the b-values of oxidation and reduction peaks equaled to 0.67 and 0.53, respectively. The results suggested surface capacitive behavior of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode material. As seen from Fig. 6e and Fig. 6f, the capacitive contribution to the entire capacity was 57 %, when the scan rate was 5 mV·s−1. With the increase of scan rate, the contribution of the diffusion controlled process decreased due to the shorter ion diffusion time into the material.

To further evaluate practical value of the as-prepared 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode materials, a hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was assembled with 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode ( Fig. 7a). Equation (S2) was used to calculate the effective mass load of AC to ensure charge conservation. Fig. 7b showed the CV curves of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF and AC in a three-electrode system at a scan rate of 30 mV·s−1. AC exhibited electric double layer capacitance characteristics in the voltage window of − 1.0 ~ 0 V while 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF showed Faradaic pseudo-capacitance behavior of in the voltage window of 0 ~ 0.5 V. The electrochemical performances of AC negative electrode were shown in Fig. S10. Before testing the capacitor performance, we at first estimated the actual operating potential. As shown in Fig. 7c, obvious polarization occurred when the potential window reached 1.7 V, so the optimal potential window was chosen as 1.6 V. In selected potential window, the assembled HSC exhibited a contorted quadrilateral shape with obvious redox peaks, implying that it had simultaneously Faradaic pseudocapacitive and EDLC-type properties at different scan rates (Fig. 7d), which resulted from pseudocapacitive positive electrode material and EDLC negative electrode, respectively [42]. This asymmetric configuration effectively expanded operation window, improved specific capacitance and enhanced specific energy. Besides, at different scan rate, the curves all kept similar shape, indicating that the supercapacitor had good stability and reversibility. Similarly, the almost symmetrical charge-discharge curves also showed that the material had high Coulombic efficiency (Fig. 7e). According to Equation (S3), the specific capacities of HSC were 176.5, 170.8, 155.8, 143.7, 136.8 C·g−1 at the current density of 1, 2, 5, 8, 10 A·g−1, respectively. It could be found that when the current density increased by 10 times, the specific capacity could still keep 77.5 % of the initial value. The Ragone plots of 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF//AC demonstrated a superior energy density of 39.22 Wh·kg−1 at 749.9 W·kg−1 (Fig. 7f). These results are much better than those reported in the literatures, such as {Mo132}-rGO//mAC (31.6 Wh·kg−1 at 207.7 W·kg−1) [43], rGO-POM//MXene (32.35 Wh·kg−1 at 7000 W·kg−1) [44], rGO-PMo1//rGO-PW12 (39 Wh·kg−1 at 658 W·kg−1) [45], ZADV@LSC//ZADV@LSC (19.7 Wh·kg−1 at 586 W·kg−1) [46], NiCo2O4 @Ni-MOF//AC (32.6 Wh·kg−1 at 349.8 W·kg−1) [47] and PMo10V2 @ZIF-67//AC (20.9 Wh·kg−1 at 702 W·kg−1) [48]. Lastly, in order to evaluate the cyclic stability, the HSC was continually carried out charging-discharging for 20000 at 10 A.g−1. As seen from Fig. 7g, after 20000 cycles, the capacitance retention was 98.4 %, and the charge-discharge curves of the first 10 cycles and the last 10 cycles basically kept the same shape, indicating excellent cyclic stability and potential application value.

In conclusion, CDs coated PMo12/Ni-MOF composite 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF was prepared by hydrothermal reaction. Due to synergistic effect among the PMo12, Ni-MOF and CDs, the prepared 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF electrode material showed a high specific capacity of 748.8 C·g−1 at 1 A·g−1. Specific coating structure assured good capacitive property and high cyclic application performance. And the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor 6CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF//AC delivered excellent cycling performance with 98.4 % capacity retention at 5 A·g−1 after 20000 cycles and the high energy density of 39.22 Wh·kg−1 at 749.9 W kg−1. This work provided a new strategy for fabricating polyoxometalate/metal organic framework based composite for supercapacitors.

Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Yuan Chen: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ning Wu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Liping Peng: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Fan Yang: Formal analysis, Software. Zhilan Pan: Formal analysis, Software. Bingqing Liu: Project administration. Hao Zhang: Investigation, Resources. Cuiliu Li: Investigation, Resources. Wu Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23), and Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.165730.
